intro: New York City is served by three separate, independent public library systems rather than one citywide agency: the New York Public Library, formed in 1895 from the consolidation of the Astor and Lenox libraries with the Tilden Trust and serving Manhattan, the Bronx, and Staten Island; the Brooklyn Public Library, established in 1896; and the Queens Public Library, whose network of neighborhood libraries formally united in 1901. Together the three systems operate well over a hundred branches and remain among the most heavily used civic institutions in the city, offering free books, programming, and digital access in every neighborhood.
